data.title: Amkor Technology: Near-Term Outlook Outweighs Positive Upside From AI-Related Demand
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for Amkor Technology (NASDAQ:AMKR) is a hold rating. I think the near-term outlook is very uncertain, especially with competitive pressures from ASE Technology and TSMC, margin headwinds from underutilization, and geopolitical risks. In addition, management’s weak 1Q25 guidance suggests further deterioration before any meaningful rebound. As such, I don’t see a strong reason to turn bullish today.

Business Overview

AMKR is an outsourced semiconductor assembly and test [OSAT] provider. It has exposure to multiple key end markets such as smartphones, high-performance computing [HPC], automotive, industrial, and consumer electronics [CE]. All these end markets are classified into 4 segments: Communications (48% of FY24 revenue); automotive, industrial and others [AIO] (18.5%); Computing (18.4%); and Consumer (15.2%).

4Q24 results update

AMKR 4Q24 results were very weak. Revenue fell to $1.63 billion, 7% y/y and 12% sequentially. Breaking down the segment performance: Communications revenue fell 26% sequentially (-27% y/y); AIO revenue declined 7% sequentially (-17% y/y), marking the seventh consecutive quarter of y/y declines; Computing revenue was a bright spot, up 15% sequentially and 50% y/y, driven by AI GPU and ARM-based PC demand; and lastly, Consumer revenue increased 10% y/y but was down 4% sequentially. Although gross margin did well, staying at 15.1% (highest among the four quarters in FY24), adj EBITDA was down 7% y/y to $302 million.

The real issue was guidance. Management’s 1Q25 revenue guidance calls for $1.275 billion (-7% y/y, -22% sequentially), which implies the downturn is still ongoing and, in fact, will get worse. Gross margin is also expected to contract to 11.5%, down 360 bps sequentially, reaching the lowest level in over a decade.

Challenges Holding Back Near-Term Growth

My biggest concern is the competitive pressures that AMKR is facing in OSAT and advanced packaging. In the OSAT industry, it is increasingly competitive, with ASE Technology (ASX) aggressively expanding its advanced packaging footprint, and management has guided for an aggressive growth outlook for 2025, showing that they are very serious about winning demand. In the advanced packaging space, the leader is clear—TSMC, which dominates CoWoS packaging for AI chips. The fact that TSMC is expanding capacity makes me wonder how much AMKR is able to capture demand from the ongoing AI investment cycle. A simple question is, why use AMKR when TSMC is already the one making the chip?

This competitive pressure has a direct impact on gross margins. It is not difficult to figure out why. For AMKR, utilization matters a lot because it reduces fixed costs per unit (per annual production), and this means high operating leverage as utilization goes up. Given that underlying demand is already poor, the add-on pressure from increased competition will further depress utilization. This also comes at a time when AMKR is ramping up its Vietnam facility (note: this ramp-up contributed to an 80 bps gross margin hit in 4Q24 and is expected to worsen to 100 bps in 1Q25), which may see the timeline to reach ideal utilization rates extended, therefore further pressuring margins.

Lastly, the geopolitical uncertainty is a big factor that could heavily weigh on AMKR's near-term stock performance, particularly regarding trade tensions and export restrictions on AI chips between the US and China. The latter has a more direct impact that is very worrisome. The recent tightening of AI chip regulations in China has already affected semiconductor supply chains, creating uncertainty for OSAT demand.

Despite the weakness I mentioned, there is a case for AMKR to see a rebound in demand. The upcoming launches of AI-enabled smartphones (like the Apple iPhone), improvement in the automotive industry (which is still in a downturn), and continued demand momentum (as seen in the Computing segment) for AI-driven packaging solutions for GPUs and HPC could drive up growth. For the latter point, as much as I am worried about alternative packaging methods, given that latent demand for AI chip packaging solutions (a rising tide lifts all boats), AMKR should still capture a portion of this AI-driven demand.

I also highlight that AMKR revenue has been 45% weighted toward 1H and 55% toward 2H. Management now expects 60% of 2025 revenue to come in 2H, which implies a 50% sequential growth rate in 2H25. If this happens as expected, the stock sentiment will turn incredibly bullish as it sets up a strong baseline for FY26 growth.

Valuation

Given the weaknesses I have stated, I believe AMKR's valuation multiple (~15x forward PE) is at a level where it is as good as it can get. Over the next twelve months, as per consensus estimates, revenue y/y growth is effectively muted, which contrasts with the ASX growth outlook or low-teens percentage. Despite this major delta, AMKR trades at similar levels to ASX (just a 1x multiple difference), and AMKR trades at its historical average multiple. As such, I think it is hard to justify AMKR trading higher than where it is today. If anything, this multiple suggests that there is room for multiples to de-rate lower, to potentially 6x forward PE (-1 standard deviation of AMKR trading range).

Conclusion

My view for AMKR remains neutral. The weak 1Q25 guidance and growing competition in OSAT and advanced packaging make me concerned about the near-term outlook (both revenue growth and margin). The current valuation is also at a level where I don’t think it will rerate higher. Until the stock gets cheaper, I would remain on the sidelines.

data.title: CGMS: An Attractive Opportunity In The Fixed Income Space
data.description:
Thesis

With risk-free rates high, fixed income has started to be an exciting area again, especially for non-leveraged exchange-traded funds. The active bond space has seen an explosion of choices in the past two years, with virtually all large asset managers launching active ETFs. We have covered the Capital Group U.S. Multi-Sector Income ETF (NYSEARCA:CGMS) before here, shortly after the fund was launched. In our initial article, we outlined the fund structure and holdings, and articulated our macro view on the name. We are going to revisit the ETF after more than a year of performance since our original article. Furthermore, we are going to look at it in light of the other choices in the space, and look at risk/reward analytics for the cohort.

Intermediate duration is appealing

While the jury is still out in respect to the long end of the curve, intermediate duration looks appealing at current rate levels. Kindly keep in mind that the Fed controls the front end of the curve via Fed Funds, but the market controls the intermediate and long end portions. The long end of the curve has been hit hard by deficit fears and the potential resurgence of inflation. From a duration standpoint, intermediate bonds have lower drawdowns even if risk-free rates move much higher. We are saying that intermediate bonds are appealing in today’s macrocycle since all-in yields are rewarding when providing for 5% to 6% all-in coupons with low duration and credit risk.

Holdings

The fund is overweight U.S. names:

From a sectoral standpoint corporate bonds account for most of the exposure in this ETF:

Corporate bonds, notes and loans represent 69% of the fund, followed by MBS bonds at 20% and ABS at 6.1%. The main risk factor here are therefore corporate spreads.

From a ratings perspective, the fund is overweight investment grade names, but has a sizable high yield composition as well:

When excluding unrated names, the below investment grade sleeve is roughly 39% of the fund holdings. To note that most of the HY names are clustered in the BB bucket, which is the least risky one for below investment grade names.

Performances and risk analytics

Let us have a look at CGMS's performance and its relative move versus a cohort of competitors:

We are comparing the fund to the iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC), the PIMCO Multisector Bond Active ETF (PYLD) and the JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE). CGMS is the clear outperformer from the cohort, and we will look at risk analytics to see if the name takes more risk than the others:

All the funds hold a mix of investment grade and high yield bonds. JPIE has the lowest duration and the lowest risk analytics as highlighted by standard deviation and volatility. The fact that the name has produced the lowest total return is thus not a surprise, since it takes the least amount of risk from the cohort.

CGMS and PYLD have similar durations, yet CGMS has managed a net superior total return, which speaks well to the fund managers and their market acumen. BINC has a slightly lower duration and slightly lower risk analytics. Kindly keep in mind though that these are active funds, and managers will shift around duration profiles and holdings as the market trades. JPIE is currently the most defensive choice here, and it has run a lower risk profile in the past years.

Advantages of active bond funds

We are of the opinion that today’s environment characterized by high volatility and lack of direction for rates is ideal for active bond funds. When yields are trending in one direction, passive funds can provide for a compelling opportunity, but when the market trades ranges with a lack of conviction, being in an active fund is a superior choice. We have seen significant fluctuations in rates in the past two years, with market participants undecided between inflationary pressures and strong economic data versus the easing monetary policy that was started by the Fed via their September 2024 rate cut.

An active fund can cut duration and credit exposures fast, thus taking shorter term views when compared to an index. Furthermore, they can bar-bell their approach to credit selection based on spread compression and market opportunities.

Macro view - corporate spreads

Corporate spreads are indeed tight, both for IG and HY bonds:

BBB spreads are currently trading at 101 bps, close to the lowest levels recorded in 2021 when rates were close to zero. While there is an argument that all-in yields are high, thus the net interest expense for corporations is higher, when at the bottom of the range there is only way direction spreads can move, and that is up.

Similarly, HY spreads for the BB sleeve are very low, even lower than the 2020/2021 period:

We think HY is even more stretched when compared to IG, considering that HY has true default probabilities embedded in the compensation.

CGMS takes mostly corporate spread risk, and via its duration we have an implied -4% drawdown for 100 bps parallel up shift in corporate spreads across IG and HY.

Conclusion

CGMS is a fixed income ETF. The name falls in the active bond category, and competes with top names from PIMCO and JPMorgan. The fund has outperformed its cohort by 200 bps in the past two years, with similar risk/reward analytics. While we are concerned about corporate spreads being stretched, CGMS' portfolio management team has proved its value via its trading acumen. We believe the most conservative choice to be JPIE for investors worried about spreads moving higher, while CGMS looks the best from the remaining names on a risk/reward basis. We like what the portfolio managers have done and believe this name is an attractive choice in today's macro environment.

data.title: Global Blue: Takeover Offer Comes At Only A Slight Premium (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment thesis

Global Blue (NYSE:GB) announced that it has entered into an agreement with Shift4 (NYSE:FOUR), according to which Shift4 will acquire 100% of GB shares. The strategic fit is clear as Shift4 aims to add GB's merchant solutions to its global payments platform, while enhancing its features by adding GB's tax refund and currency conversion capabilities. The acquisition is expected to be closed in Q3 this year.

GB also posted impressive preliminary figures for Q3, which puts the company on track to deliver FY25 Adjusted EBITDA at the upper end of management's guidance. I would point out that I have remained bullish on GB stock since May last year when shares were trading below $5. I even reiterated my bullish stance following GB's Q2 earnings release. In this write-up I share why I think the acquisition is likely to go through despite the acquisition price only coming at a modest premium of 20% to yesterday's closing price. I am therefore downgrading GB stock to a Hold as shares are now trading close to the acquisition price.

Reviewing preliminary Q3 results

Global Blue announced preliminary figures for Q3 FY25 along with the announcement of the acquisition offer. Revenue was up 20% year over year, reaching €131 million, while Adjusted EBITDA was €52 million, up 31% year over year. Given that Adjusted EBITDA in H1 FY25 was €101.7 million, this puts the company on track to exceed €200 million in Adjusted EBITDA for FY25, which is closer to the upper range of management's guidance of €185 million to €200 million.

The Tax Free Shopping Business Update for January published by the company showed that sales continued to be strong, with 19% year over year Sales in Store growth in continental Europe and a corresponding 46% growth in Asia Pacific.

Analyzing the acquisition price and the implied valuation

The acquisition price of $7.5 per share reflects a 20% premium over the closing price on the 18th of February. This puts the company's market cap at $1.82 billion, based on 242 million shares outstanding, after also including the 40 million preferred shares. Given the company's net debt at the end of Q2 of $520 million, this implies a EV/Adjusted EBITDA valuation multiple of 11.2, based on €200 million expected for FY25, which translates to around $210 million, assuming a FX rate of 1€=$1.05.

In order to arrive at the expected FCF, I deduct $40 million in interest expenses, capex of $45 million and tax payments of approximately $30 million from the expected adjusted EBITDA figure. This leads to FCF of $95 million, implying a Price/FCF multiple of 19.1. If we exclude interest costs, to get the true FCF generation potential for the business, the valuation multiple on a Price/FCF basis drops to just 13.4, based on FCF of $135 million.

Even after the takeover premium, this still represents a very attractive valuation for this resilient business, which has a dominant market position in tax free shopping, and is expected to grow revenue at double digit rates, with potential margin upside.

Expectations looking ahead

Shift4 has $1.42 billion in cash on hand, while also having access to a 364-day $1.8 billion bridge loan facility that was entered in connection with the transaction. This makes it unlikely that they will have any issue with respect to funding. Furthermore, Shift4 estimates that approximately 90% of the owners of the GB's issued and outstanding common shares and preferred shares are willing to tender their shares. This includes large owners like Silverlake and Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) which have ownership stakes of 63% and 9%, respectively. Given the low public float of just 15%, I believe it is very likely that Shift4 ends up getting the minimum 90% acceptance that is required.

Conclusion

GB posted strong Q3 results with Adjusted EBITDA on track to be close to the upper range of full year guidance. Its valuation remains attractive at the takeover price of $7.5 per share and while the acquisition price only reflects a modest premium, the deal is likely to go through, with a low likelihood for a competing bid to arrive at this stage. With shares now trading at close to the acquisition price, I am downgrading the stock from Buy to a Hold.

data.title: Private Equity, Venture Capital Flows To Biotechnology Lowest Since 2019
data.description:
In 2024, global private equity and venture capital transaction value and volume in biotechnology declined to their lowest levels since 2019, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

Compared to 2023, transaction value declined by 11% to $20.28 billion, and the number of deals dropped to 777 from 894.

Rising interest rates affecting investment valuations, increasing product development costs and a complex regulatory environment have contributed to the decline, Dan Shoenholz, US healthcare and life sciences PE leader at EY-Parthenon, told Market Intelligence.

However, the sector showed some positive data in 2024, with private equity-backed biotechnology transaction values increasing each quarter throughout the year.

The US and Canada had 281 deals in 2024, the highest among all regions, followed by the Asia-Pacific region with 264 deals.

The aggregate transaction value of private equity in biotech companies in the US and Canada reached $13.33 billion, followed by Europe at $4.41 billion and Asia-Pacific at $2.45 billion.

Largest biotech deals in 2024

All top 10 private equity-backed biotechnology deals in 2024 involved companies based in the US and Canada.

Obesity treatment therapies provider Kailera Therapeutics Inc.'s $400 million round of funding, led by Bain Capital Life Sciences Investors LLC, Lyra Capital LLC, Atlas Venture LP and RTW Investments LP, was the largest deal in 2024.

The second-largest deal was Arsenal Biosciences Inc.'s $325.4 million series C round of funding, which included participation from Arch Venture Partners LP, Mwg Management Ltd., Regeneron Ventures, NVentures LLC, Luma Group Advisor LLC, funds and accounts advised by T. Rowe Price Associates Inc., and Rock Springs Capital Management LP. Existing investors Parker Institute for Cancer Immunotherapy, SoftBank Vision Fund II-2 LP, Bristol-Myers Squibb Co., Westlake Village BioPartners, Kleiner Perkins Caufield & Byers, Byers Capital Management LLC and Hitachi Ventures GmbH also participated.

Within healthcare, biotechnology was the second-most invested industry, while pharmaceuticals had the highest deal value total of $28.15 billion. However, biotechnology stood out with the highest number of deals.

Outlook

A shrinking pool of public biopharma companies is increasing demand for strong privately held biotech companies, said Peter Kolchinsky, founder and managing partner of RA Capital Management LP, a life sciences-focused private equity firm that participated in fifty private financings in the biotech industry in 2024.

"The most promising science and the best companies are still attracting funding at competitive valuations," Kolchinsky said.

Carter Neild, managing partner at OrbiMed Advisors LLC, said, "lower interest rates and a pro-innovation FDA" are expected to support investment in the sector.

Lower rates can provide a cheaper cost of capital for expensive R&D funding and tend to make riskier assets, like biotech startups, more attractive.

Artificial intelligence is another factor. AI could significantly impact the biotech sector, and companies are exploring its uses. Last month, the FDA released draft guidance on AI in drug development, informed by over 500 AI-related submissions since 2016, highlighting AI's expanding role in drug development.

Private equity investors may view AI as an opportunity to accelerate drug discovery and the lengthy approval processes, potentially leading to faster market entry and profits, said John Newton, private equity partner at Ropes & Gray LLP. However, AI's practical application is still developing, with regulators working to ensure its effective use while maintaining drug safety and efficacy, he added.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Texas Capital Bancshares: Preferred Shares Yielding 6.7%
data.description:
Introduction

Texas Capital Bancshares (NASDAQ:TCBI) is a regional bank with a major presence in the metropolitan areas of Texas. In addition to common shares, the bank offers a preferred share (NASDAQ:TCBIO) with a current dividend yield of 6.7%. I’ve been long the preferred shares since the regional banking crisis and visited the bank’s performance last fall. Today, the bank continues to perform well, and fixed income investors should be prepared to continue to reap long-term income from the bank’s preferred shares.

Texas Capital Bancshares Financial Results

The Federal Reserve’s easing of interest rates during the fourth quarter caused Texas Capital’s yields to pull back. Asset yields dropped 37 basis points from 5.96% to 5.59% in the fourth quarter. Unfortunately, the bank’s borrowing yields declined by less than the asset yields, dropping from 4.7% to 4.37% in the fourth quarter. While the net interest spread declined, and has been on a rough trajectory since 2022, the bank has managed its leverage well and as a result, its net interest margin remains above pandemic levels.

As with yields, Texas Capital Bancshares saw the same changes to interest income and expense during the fourth quarter. Interest income declined by $15 million, while interest expense dropped by only $4 million. While the $11 million decline in net interest income (interest income less interest expense) may concern investors, net interest income remains higher than during the latter part of 2023, early 2024, and the pandemic era.

Changes in Loans and Deposits

After several quarters of loan declines, lending for Texas Capital Bancshares surged in 2024 with the bank posting three strong quarters of lending growth. In the fourth quarter, loans grew by 3.3% and for the year, lending grew by more than 10%. The bank did have a strong year for deposit growth, despite losing 2.4% of deposits in the fourth quarter. For the year, deposits grew by nearly 13%, far above what the typical commercial bank saw.

With the quarterly inconsistencies in loan and deposit growth, Texas Capital Bancshares’ loan to deposit ratio has moved between 85 and 90% over the past couple of years. With lending growth outpacing deposits in the fourth quarter, the loan-to-deposit ratio rose to 88%. The loan to deposit ratio indicates how dependent the bank is on external financing to fund loan growth. In the case of Texas Capital Bancshares, while the ratio is higher than average, it is on par with what investors can expect from a regional bank. In fact, during 2024, the bank made significant progress towards reducing its external borrowing, a move that should help earnings in 2025.

Risks to Texas Capital Bancshares

Texas Capital Bancshares’ loans are mostly classified as either commercial, mortgage finance, or commercial real estate. Commercial loans are nearly half of the bank’s loan portfolio and tend to be comprised of shorter-term loans, but the other two types have longer duration commitments and are subject to performance issues should the economy deteriorate.

Texas Capital’s nonperforming loans did grow from $81 million to $111 million in 2024 and with a smaller than average allowance for credit losses, there is a risk of poor loan performance bleeding into earnings. Since it is likely that the bank will need to increase its loan allowance in 2025, I’m not taking a position in its common shares. With the preferred shares sitting senior to the common shares, preferred share investors should not notice the volatility as much as common shareholders would.

Conclusion

Despite the risks to Texas Capital Bancshares, preferred share investors should experience less volatility to any problems that arise. The bank’s preferred share dividend is also low, which allows it to trade at a discount, and makes the possibility of them being called at par highly unlikely for the foreseeable future. Income investors can earn 6.7% dividend yield off the preferred shares with little risk of impairment, making Texas Capital Bancshares an attractive preferred share option for investors.

data.title: Vinci: Back In Favor In 2025
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome The Zen Trader as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

After a punishing year for the share price, owing to early elections in France which led to widespread concerns about rising taxes, Vinci (OTCPK:VCISF) stands to regain the market's favour this year thanks to its unabated earnings growth, its superior cash generation profile, and a valuation which now looks very undemanding.

Tough 2024 for the stock, not so much for the business

Vinci, the French infrastructure and construction company, which over the past decade has built itself up to become the global industry leader, closed 2024 down 12% as the stock got dragged into broader outflows from the French market and particularly stocks perceived as "domestic" (Vinci still earns just under half of its revenues in France). This taints an otherwise very respectable track record, annualising around 11% on average over the prior 5 years (and that includes an 18% down move in Covid-stricken 2020).

Nonetheless, the operational outperformance of the business has continued undeterred. Vinci presented its 2024 results on February 7th, surprising the market with a solid bottom-line beat of 3% and a much greater beat 59% beat on free cash flow, arguably the company's main KPI. Once again, Vinci has proven itself as the industry leader not only in size, but also in operational excellence, consistently delivering results above market expectations.

This outperformance of market expectations is precisely the feature which, I believe, has enabled the stock to deliver double-digit compound annual returns for the past decade, and is set to bring the stock back into favour this year as French political risk settles.

Upside to consensus estimates supports a bullish thesis

The negative sentiment that has surrounded the stock in the past year seems to have percolated down to analysts' forecasts too, which to me seems unjustified. In the short term, growth is supported by very solid fundamentals such as the continued recovery in traffic across its airports operations, the largest privately-held such portfolio in the world, and rising global trends in energy contracting, where Vinci stands to benefit as one of the leading players in the field.

Airports are likely to clock in another strong year. EBIT grew 24% in 2024, but that was helped by a major tariff increase at Portuguese ANA, Vinci's largest airport asset, as well as the first-time partial consolidation of Edinburgh airport which was acquired in June. For 2025, I forecast a growth rate that is half of 2024 but still attractive at 12% year-on-year, driven by steady traffic growth amplified by the ongoing recovery at some peripheral airports like Cambodia, and progressive improvement in EBIT margins through a combination of higher traffic (operating leverage) and increased retail spend, a trend that is consistent across global airports.

At Vinci, energy contracting is undertaken by two businesses. Vinci Energies is the company's legacy contracting business, with decades of experience in delivering energy solutions to its growing client base, and more recently benefitting from increasingly stringent regulations around energy efficiency and emissions. Then there is recently acquired Cobra Industrial Services, a high-performing energy infrastructure developer focused on big-ticket, high-margin projects in fast-growing markets like electricity transmission and offshore wind. Vinci Energies grew operating profits by 9% in 2024 and margins by 20bps, and I expect them to deliver a similar growth in 2025. Cobra, on the other hand, grew EBIT 13% in 2024, with margins expanding 30bps. Again, thanks to a growing order book as well as improving mix in contracts with an increasing bias towards high complexity, high margin projects, I expect this progressive improvement in profitability to continue, forecasting slightly lower but still double-digit headline EBIT growth and an equal level of margin expansion.

As for the company's other key divisions, I expect French Autoroutes to remain flattish year-on-year, with a potentially major recovery ahead in 2026 when the infrastructure tax imposed in France last year, weighing c.10% on the division's operating profit, should expire. Similarly, Vinci Construction should experience modest growth, in the 3-5% range at EBIT level, given mature margins and a stable order book.

Altogether, I expect this to imply a return to Vinci's historical bottom-line growth rate of roughly 10% per annum. This is significantly better than the market's anaemic current expectation of <3% EPS growth, which, I believe, will necessarily need to be revised up over the course of the year as operational performance comes through.

(Free) Cash is King

Operational outperformance will inevitably translate into progressively higher free cash flow generation. Here, Vinci is set to surprise the market to an even greater extent, in my view.

The market continues to be in disbelief at Vinci's ability to generate cash despite three consecutive years of major free cash flow beats. In their usual fashion, market analysts today predict that Vinci's annual free cash flow in 2025 will fall c.30% compared to the level achieved in 2024. Just like with earnings, this market expectation stands to be revised up meaningfully over the course of this year. Not only because it is clearly at odds with both the business' steady organic growth discussed above, but more importantly because it neglects the consistently accelerating growth in order book across the contract businesses which, through prepayments, has led to meaningful progressive improvements in the company's working capital in recent years.

Strong buffer for incremental growth

The magnitude of the cash flow in the context of Vinci's balance sheet is also worth a note. Last year, the company generated €7.7bn in free cash. This year, consensus expects €5.2bn but, as mentioned, the real figure is likely to be closer to last year's level. Vinci's net debt, which at 2024 close stood at €22bn (less than 2x the company's EBITDA), pales in comparison to the underlying cash generation, which could easily repay the company's entire indebtedness in under four years. Given this naturally de-leveraging business model, Vinci has aggressively deployed capital into acquisitions in recent years, which an impeccable track record at generating earnings accretion. While never guaranteed, this means that M&A may further enhance the company's growth prospects beyond the underlying organic growth drivers discussed above.

Valuation: Up to 20% upside

Summarising the points discussed above, Vinci stock stands to see about 7% positive EPS revisions this year. Even at a flat multiple, that would be a decent return given the company's low intrinsic volatility of earnings. The stock's current valuation, however, is arguably subdued by historical standards, looking at both earnings and cash flows, the two most important metrics when evaluating infrastructure stocks, which in my view further room for re-rating. In a context where the stock trades at 12.5x forward P/E, a trough valuation compared to its own average multiple of around 15x during the last 10 years, a re-rating back to the historical norm would imply 20% upside on the shares. Similarly, from a cash flow perspective, the stock now trades at a 12% yield, far off the much more sensible c.8% it has traded at over the past decade, and still higher than the more conservative 10% it has averaged since Covid hit in 2020. Moving back to the recent historical average would also here imply a 20% up move. Within the infrastructure sector, Vinci trades at a very meaningful discount to peers such as Ferrovial (FER)(45x P/E and 4% FCFY) and Getlink (OTCPK:GRPTF)(34x P/E and 6.5% FCFY), suggesting that its underlying assets and the cash flows that they generate are heavily discounted, potentially due to large size and relative complexity of the overall business. The stock now trades at only a mild premium to its closest peer, Eiffage (OTCPK:EFGSY)(8.5x P/E and 33% FCFY), when compared to the historical spread between the two stocks. That is easily justified however by looking at Vinci's meaningfully higher exposures to growth engines such as the airports and energy contracting businesses.

Conclusion: Back in favour in 2025

As such, I expect that Vinci could close the year at around €130/share, reflecting a 20% return on the shares that currently trade around €108/share. Considering it was already trading above 120 this time last year, that does not seem far-fetched. Since the start of the year, the stock has only made back about half of what it lost over the course of 2024, meaning that it's not too late to get involved. I would buy the stock here and hold it through the end of the year, letting positive consensus earnings revisions, catalysed by Vinci's ongoing delivery on growth and cash generation, do their part.

The risks

The main risk affecting Vinci this year continues to be French politics and public finances. France has passed a budget last week which, at least in its current form, would envisage additional taxation for French corporates in the short term. The news is out, so it would appear to be in the price, but the enactment of the taxes may dampen sentiment in the market and, depending on its magnitude, may detract from the short-term earnings revisions which are at the core of the investment thesis.

Additionally, this is a recurring risk. While France may have squared the circle on this year's budget, the risk of further pressure on public finances translating into risk of incremental taxation will likely re-emerge later this year (EU countries are required to present draft budgets in Brussels by October 20th). I am particularly concerned about the infrastructure tax, already discussed above in the section about the Autoroutes division, which currently raises more than half a billion Euros per year, and while supposedly a temporary measure expiring in 2026, I can see a scenario where this gets extended indefinitely to plug the country's fiscal hole.

From a company-specific perspective, a management transition is currently underway, with Chairman and CEO Xavier Huillard stepping down from executive duties in April 2025. This has long been planned and anticipated, but always carries some risk. While I do not expect new management to go down the traditional "kitchen sinking" route, lowering the starting point for their financial outperformance (Vinci has far too strong a reputation for that to be feasible), I can see how a more ambitious new management team may decide to put greater stress on the company's balance sheet to accelerate growth through acquisitions. These may cast doubt on value creation if not executed seamlessly, and in particular may raise eyebrows if acquiring large assets at multiples which will almost certainly exceed the stock's current valuation in the market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kennametal: Visible Near-Term Weakness
data.description:
Investment overview

I give a hold rating for Kennametal (NYSE:KMT) as I see no near-term catalysts for recovery and expect continued weakness in its core Metal Cutting segment. The ongoing weakness in industrial demand, combined with rising input costs and weaker-than-expected guidance, signals poor near-term performance. While I believe that KMT’s valuation is unjustified, my lack of a strong view on the macro environment held me back from giving a sell rating.

Business description

KMT operates two main segments. The first segment is Metal Cutting, which supplies cutting tools used in manufacturing. The second segment is Infrastructure, which provides durable components for energy, mining, and construction applications. Of the two, metal cutting represents ~63% of total revenue as of FY24, and infrastructure represents the rest. However, in terms of adj EBIT, metal cutting represents a bigger piece of the pie at 77%.

2Q25 Earnings Show Signs of More Trouble Ahead

KMT 2Q25 results paint a negative picture of near-term performance. Total revenue came in at $482 million, down 3% y/y on a reported basis and 6% on an organic sales basis. This marked the fifth consecutive quarter of negative organic growth, showing that demand weakness is not just a temporary blip but a near-term structural challenge. By segments, the Metal Cutting segment was particularly weak, with sales declining 4% y/y to $298 million on a reported basis and down 7% on an organic basis. The infrastructure segment, which reported revenue of $184 million, was flattish on a y/y basis. This may seem positive, but I note that the segment saw one-time benefits from a $2 million insurance recovery and a $2 million Inflation Reduction Act credit. Without these, segment performance would have been negative y/y.

The only positive takeaway was that gross margin improved slightly to 30.1%, up 190 bps y/y. However, not all of this expansion flowed into adj EBIT margin expansion. Adj EBIT margin only expanded by 90 bps y/y from 6% to 6.9%. KMT’s core segment—metal cutting—saw adj EBIT margin fall by 240 bps y/y to 6%. All of these led to KMT reporting adj EPS of $0.25, missing consensus estimates of $0.258.

Metal Cutting Segment Continues to Deteriorate

My biggest concern is that KMT’s core segment—metal cutting segment—performance continues to deteriorate with no signs of stabilization. The 7% organic sales decline in 2Q25 was worse than the -4% organic decline seen in 1Q25, suggesting that the downturn is accelerating rather than stabilizing. The problem is, I do not see any signs of a potential rebound in the near term. While aerospace and defense did very well, up 14% y/y, it only accounts for ~10-12% of total revenue. This was not enough to offset steep declines in other key markets. For instance:

  • General engineering (54% of metal cutting segment sales), which is tied to overall industrial production trends, fell 4% y/y.
  • Transportation saw a steep 9% y/y drop.
  • Earthworks declined 7% y/y. The lower coal prices should continue to result in reduced capital spending among key customers.
  • The energy segment was up just 1% y/y, but this was primarily due to project timing. As such, no positive momentum is seen.

All these declines are not isolated to one region. KMT is seeing weakness across North America, EMEA, and Asia-Pacific, indicating that macro pressures are weighing on industrial production across the board (in the Americas, EMEA, and APAC). The problem is that there is little reason to expect these trends to reverse in the near term. Manufacturing PMIs remain weak globally, and mining capex investments are unlikely to rebound. Without a clear catalyst for recovery, I think it is very difficult to see how KMT can return to meaningful organic growth anytime soon. EMEA, in particular, is one area I am monitoring because its manufacturing PMI readings remain in contraction territory. Given that Europe is ~30% of KMT’s total sales (as of 2Q25), it remains a big headwind to KMT’s ability to drive up organic growth.

Down the P&L for this segment, another concern is the rising tungsten prices, which is a key input for KMT cutting tools. While management has implemented price increases to offset inflation, I don’t think they are able to effectively pass through more of such cost inflation moving forward given the poor underlying demand trends. If raw material costs continue to rise, I see further downside potential for the metal cutting adj EBIT margin—which already fell from low teens in FY24 to just 6% in 2Q25.

FY25 Guidance Cut Signals Little Potential for Near-Term Turnaround

If the above is not enough to convince you that the near-term outlook is poor, management's downward revision in guidance should convince you. Management has cut FY25 guidance, now expecting revenue of $1.95 billion–$2 billion, down from the prior range of $2 billion–$2.1 billion. Adjusted EPS was also cut significantly by 13% at the midpoint. The way I see it, this clearly sends a signal that management is not seeing any positive demand trends, which means a near-term turnaround is unlikely.

Valuation

For a business that is facing major headwinds in demand, has cut guidance, and has little to no visibility to a turnaround, it is amazing that the stock trades at its historical average of 17x NTM PE. I believe KMT should trade closer to the low end of its trading range (12x) given the horrible outlook and poor guidance. For historical reference, KMT traded at 12x NTM PE in late 2023 when organic growth turned negative (to -0.4%). Given that KMT organic growth is likely to stay at this negative mid-single-digit percentage level, I don’t see a strong justification that KMT should trade at a premium. As such, my view is that there is more downside than upside at the current valuation.

The reason I am now recommending a sell rating is because I do not have a strong view on the macro-outlook. It is especially difficult to pinpoint when the global macro will turn around. If it turns around in the near term, KMT could see a strong recovery, and that could justify the current valuation.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for KMT. My belief is that KMT is facing weakness in its key end markets, and management's FY25 guidance cuts reinforced my view that the near-term outlook will be challenging. Hence, I struggle to see a strong case for any upside. That said, a macroeconomic recovery could drive up the stock price. As such, I did not give a sell rating but a hold rating.

data.title: Blackbaud: Growth Rates Deteriorate Further In Q4, Invest Elsewhere
data.description:
In early 2025, news headlines have been swamped with President Trump's raft of executive orders. The changes brought by the new administration have been sweeping and broad, but beyond the remaking of the executive branch, Trump has also succeeded at enacting cultural changes across corporate boardrooms, particularly by pushing companies down DEI and ESG initiatives that sprouted during the pandemic period.

Amid this backdrop, Blackbaud (NASDAQ:BLKB) has suffered. The company has a broad suite of tools for its education, not-for-profit, and public sector clientele, but a major area for growth had been providing software for ESG-style initiatives in corporations. Stacked on top of a tough macro environment, the company's growth has slid to near-zero, as evidenced by its recent Q4 earnings release. Shares of Blackbaud dropped ~10% after the print, as investors lost patience for this fading story.

Major risks cloud this company as growth slows to a halt

I last wrote a bearish note on Blackbaud in November, when the stock was trading closer to $85 per share. Since then, Blackbaud has eroded a further ~20% (we note that the company's decision to not take an $80 take-private offer now appears unwise), while growth rates have soured further. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my sell rating on Blackbaud.

There are a number of risk factors that investors need to keep in mind here, beyond just the unfavorable social and political climate for its ESG growth initiatives. To me, Blackbaud's growth strategy is flawed. Historically, the company has relied heavily on M&A for its growth. The legacy of this strategy is a very constrained balance sheet: the company has just $67.6 million of cash left on its balance sheet, counterbalanced by $1.08 billion of debt. This ~$1 billion of net debt, meanwhile, stands at a ~10x leverage ratio against its TTM adjusted EBITDA of just $102 million (note that commercial banks typically consider a ~3x leverage ratio to be a highly leveraged, risky company).

Meanwhile, its acquisitions also don't have a great track record of panning out: it just sold one of its education subsidiaries, EVERFI, and took a major ~$0.4 billion impairment charge as a result.

We also note that Blackbaud's revenue growth relies on an updated business model and pricing strategy that effectively amounts to a pricing increase. The company is now renewing its customers' contracts to three-year terms (versus a mix of term lengths previously), implementing a "mid to high teens" rate increase at renewal with a "mid to high single digit" increase in both years 2 and 3.

Needless to say, these are onerous terms. Organizations are struggling to keep budgets slim in today's macro environment. If we take the example of a 15%, 7%, and 7% price increase over a three-year timeframe, a customer would be paying ~30% more for Blackbaud by the third year: to me, this may be a driver that causes more customer churn. Blackbaud's failure to grow beyond the low single digits suggests that churn may be outweighing the benefits of its price increases.

All in all, this is a company on very shaky footing - and in my view, the post-earnings crash has quite a ways to go still.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Blackbaud's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Blackbaud's revenue grew at just 2.4% y/y to $302.2 million, missing Wall Street's expectations of $303.4 million (+2.8% y/y) while also decelerating a point from Q3's 3.3% growth rate. We note that organic growth was stronger when excluding the impact of the sale of EVERFI, standing at 3.2% y/y - still, this is not exactly the kind of growth we'd expect or hope for, especially from a company that is implementing rather extreme price increases.

The bridge below helps to explain the drivers behind Blackbaud's poor growth:

The company's social sector drove growth, both through the company's implementation of price increases (reflected in contractual recurring revenue) as well as higher take rates on transactional recurring revenue. Take rates represent the percentage that Blackbaud is earning from helping its clients process gifts and other transactions, and higher take rates is yet another element of a price increase - in other words, this is not a growth lever that can be pulled in perpetuity. Meanwhile, we note that the company's corporate sector revenue declined -14% y/y, which is fitting in an era when many companies are slashing ESG initiatives.

Meanwhile, on the profitability front, the company has been able to barely hang on its current margin profile. Adjusted EBITDA in Q4 rose 3.1% y/y to $102.2 million, with margins of 33.8% improving just 20bps y/y versus 33.6% in the year-ago Q4.

The company is expecting profitability to improve by 170bps y/y in FY25, which I believe to be optimistic. Its current pace of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion already factors in the macroeconomic headwinds as well as benefits from recent price increases. Yes, the company will save opex from divesting the underperforming EVERFI segment, but this may not be enough to offset inflation pressures and the possibility of added churn. I'd prefer to see additional cost-saving measures on Blackbaud's part to become more comfortable with this name.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $72, Blackbaud trades at a market cap of $3.66 billion. After we net off the $67.6 million of cash and $1.08 billion of debt on the company's balance sheet, Blackbaud's resulting enterprise value is $4.67 billion.

Meanwhile, for the current fiscal year FY25, Blackbaud has guided to $1.115-$1.125 billion in revenue (~5% y/y growth), $190 million in midpoint FCF (a 17% FCF margin), and $4.16-$4.35 in pro forma EPS (2-7% EPS growth, which also is supported by the company's buybacks, with ~$0.6 billion remaining on its current authorization).

I'm skeptical that the company can achieve ~5% organic revenue growth when it exited Q4 at a growth rate that is two points lower. But if we take the company's outlook at face value, Blackbaud trades at multiples of:

  • 4.2x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 25x EV/FY25 FCF
  • 17x P/E

To me, despite a mountain of operational and fundamental risks that Blackbaud faces, the stock isn't even quite a value stock, with its P/E multiple just barely below the broader S&P 500 (and we note that the company's earnings multiple would be bloated higher if we account for its debt, which is why its ~25x FCF multiple is a more accurate representation).

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: January's $10 Billion M&A Deals And Bids Span Sectors
data.description:
A US power plant operator and a pharmaceutical company were among the M&A targets in transactions valued at more than $10 billion that were announced in January.

There were five potential $10 billion-plus transactions initiated worldwide in January, excluding terminated bids, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. That matched the three-month totals recorded in the second and third quarters of 2024. The fourth quarter of 2024 saw twice as many $10 billion-plus deal announcements, with 10.

The January megadeals included transactions in the energy, financials, pharmaceuticals and materials sectors, signaling a broad-based large-cap M&A market appetite.

Announced deals

On Jan. 10, renewable electricity company Constellation Energy Generation LLC (CEG) agreed to acquire power plant operator Calpine Corp. in a transaction valued at $29.39 billion at the time of the announcement. A few days later, pharmaceutical giant Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and biopharmaceutical company Intra-Cellular Therapies Inc. (ITCI) signed a definitive agreement confirming Johnson & Johnson's proposed $13.65 billion purchase of Intra-Cellular Therapies on Jan. 13.

The combination of Constellation Energy Generation and Calpine Corp. would create the US's largest clean energy provider, according to the merger announcement. The deal adds geographic diversity to Maryland-based Constellation Energy Generation's coverage by bringing Texas-based Calpine Corp.'s customers under its umbrella.

Johnson & Johnson's acquisition of Intra-Cellular Therapies, which focuses on the development and commercialization of therapeutics for central nervous system disorders, demonstrates the company's commitment to research on neuropsychiatric and neurodegenerative disorders, Chairman and CEO Joaquin Duato said in Johnson & Johnson's press release announcing the deal.

Australian materials companies Aston Minerals Ltd. (OTCPK:WMNNF) and Torque Metals Ltd. agreed to a merger of equals Jan. 28 valued at $13.09 billion at the announcement, including the acquisition of outstanding options, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. According to the press release announcing the deal, Torque Metals will acquire 100% of the fully paid ordinary shares in Aston Minerals and 100% of the unlisted Aston Minerals options. Excluding the options transaction, the deal's total consideration to shareholders at the announcement was AU$14.4 million.

Placed bids

Multiple potential acquirers placed large bids during the month of January. Software company QXO Inc. (QXO) offered to acquire materials distribution company Beacon Roofing Supply Inc. (BECN) in a tender offer with an implied transaction value of $11.55 billion at the time of announcement on Jan. 15. Italian bank Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena SpA (OTCPK:BMDPF) submitted a $13.73 all-stock takeover offer to its peer Mediobanca Banca di Credito Finanziario SpA (OTCPK:MDIBF) on Jan. 24. Both bids were rejected by the targets' boards.

QXO commenced a tender offer to purchase all outstanding Beacon Roofing Supply shares for $124.25 per share in cash. Beacon Roofing Supply confirmed the receipt of the offer Nov. 11, 2024, and rejected it. The board stated in a press release that QXO's bid severely undervalued Beacon Roofing Supply for growth and future value creation.

QXO responded with a letter to Beacon Roofing Supply on Feb. 10, stating the bid represents a 37% premium to Beacon Roofing Supply's 90-day unaffected share price of $91.02 per share as of Nov. 15, 2024.

Monte dei Paschi's bid to purchase Mediobanca comes amid a pickup in bank M&A in Italy. The Italian government backed the bid, with both the country's Prime Minister, Giorgia Meloni, and Economy Minister Giancarlo Giorgetti saying it would strengthen Italy's economy and banking system. Mediobanca's board rejected Monte dei Paschi's bid on Jan. 28, stating in a press release that the board found the offer "devoid of industrial and financial rationale and... therefore destructive."

If the proposed purchase was successful, it would be the largest Italian bank deal of the decade, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NewLake Capital Partners: 11% Yield, Discount To Book Value Make Up For Risks
data.description:
NewLake Capital Partners (OTCQX:NLCP), a cannabis REIT, has been performing poorly since my last Buy rating on the stock on October 25, 2024, with a total return of -18.4%. However, the business was still doing just fine as of Q3, which is its most recently reported earnings period. Now, the stock is trading under its tangible book value, and it yields nearly 11%, presenting an attractive opportunity for dividend investors.

At the same time, I believe tenant risk is moderate, as the top two tenants make up a large portion of revenues, and several of NLCP's tenants don't have ideal financials. Case in point, NLCP has had problems with two of its tenants, as they struggled to make rent payments in recent months.

But then I also consider that some of this downside potential is likely baked into the valuation and that the company has a high-quality business model, operating in a growing industry. You can see more of my thoughts on its business (as well as thoughts on the SAFER Banking Act) in my previous article. There's also the fact that its high-yielding dividend is covered by AFFO, at least for now.

After balancing these pros and cons, I'm still leaning bullish on NLCP stock, giving it a Buy rating.

Two Of NLCP's Tenants Are In Trouble

Two of NLCP's tenants have been struggling to pay their leases recently. Revolutionary Clinics is one of these tenants, making up 5.7% of the NewLake Capital Partner's annualized base rent (ABR) as of the third quarter.

The company paid only 50% of its contractual rent in Q3 and continued paying the reduced amount into October as well. We'll get more updates on the status of the company after NLCP reports earnings on March 6. But for now, things aren't looking great because Revolutionary's main lender "asked a judge to place the company in receivership," per greenmarketreport.com.

Next, there's Calypso, which made up 6.5% of NLCP's annualized base rent as of Q3. Calypso didn't pay rent for both September and October 2024. To try to improve the situation, NLCP used about $299,000 from Calypso's escrow deposit to cover the missed rent from September. It also suspended its obligation to "fund the remaining improvement allowance of approximately $1.0 million until all outstanding rent is paid, and the escrow deposit is replenished."

On the bright side, in the Q3 earnings call, NewLake's management said the company is confident in the demand for Calypso's facility in Pennsylvania, which indicates to me that they may not struggle too much to find another tenant. Here's the quote: "Calypso is evaluating all options for the business, and we are confident there is demand for the high-quality facility in a state with positive tailwinds."

Considering that Pennsylvania was the 6th-biggest cannabis retail market in the US (according to this article from September) despite only having medical-related sales, perhaps it's true that there is demand for the facility.

Taking A Look At The Rest Of NLCP's Tenants

I believe NLCP's tenant profile presents above-average risk, but this should come to no surprise since it's a cannabis REIT. Let's analyze each tenant briefly.

The biggest tenant presents a mixed picture: Its biggest tenant is Curaleaf (OTCPK:CURLF), which represented 22.9% of annualized base rent. That much rent coming from one company is already a risk, but let's take a look into Curaleaf's financials.

Curaleaf's interest coverage ratio is 0.4x, meaning that its EBIT doesn't cover interest payments. That's a red flag, but there's a silver lining.

While its interest coverage ratio is 0.4x, its free cash flow is positive and expected to improve in 2025. Curaleaf's TTM free cash flow comes in at around $47.6 million, and this figure is expected to reach $67 million for FY2024 and $114.5 million for 2025, per the consensus of two analysts on tikr.com.

Thus, while I'd like to see the interest coverage ratio improve, Curaleaf should be able to pay its rent if its free cash flow stays positive. Further, with its market cap being rather large for a cannabis stock ($1.1 billion), the firm is likely big enough to be able to raise a notable amount of money via an equity raise if it has to. This can help with rent/debt payments.

The 2nd-biggest tenant is not so bad: Cresco Labs (OTCQX:CRLBF) represents 13% of ABR, so it's worth paying attention to. The good news is that this company doesn't have a problem with its interest coverage ratio. Cresco Labs' TTM interest coverage ratio is 2.0x, and it has been around that level for a few quarters now. Its EBIT is expected to be $114 million for 2024 and $113.65 million for 2025. That's a good buffer when you consider that Cresco Labs' TTM interest expense is $60.162 million.

This company is FCF positive as well. Its TTM free cash flow is $77.6 million, and based on the estimates of five analysts, FCF of $96.7 million is expected for FY2024 and $43 million for 2025. Assuming these estimates are at least somewhat accurate, then I'm not too worried about Cresco Labs.

Trulieve Cannabis (OTCQX:TCNNF), the third-largest tenant: Trulieve represents 10.8% of ABR. My thoughts on Trulieve are similar to those of Cresco Labs. It has positive FCF ($283.1 million in the past 12 months), and its interest coverage ratio of 1.8x is not horrible. Its free cash flow is expected to dip to $129.3 million for FY2024 and then rise to $156.6 million by the end of this year. This means the company should be able to pay rent.

The fourth-largest publicly traded tenant doesn't look healthy, but there's a chance: The next tenant is The Cannabist Company (OTCQX:CBSTF) (CBST:CA), representing 8.2% of ABR. This is probably the worst one so far, as its TTM EBIT is ~$300 thousand, but its interest expense was $44.9 million. Further, its free cash flow is negative, at -$24.147 million for the past 12 months. Adding to this shaky financial position, its cash and short-term investments are only $32.68 million, while the total debt comes in at $478.2 million when counting capital leases.

The silver lining is that 2025 free cash flow for the company is forecast at $16.2 million. However, that's just according to one analyst, so it's a big "we'll see."

The remaining tenants: Now that I've some of the bigger tenants out of the way, let's briefly talk about the smaller ones. I will ignore the private companies, as there's not enough information on them.

Acreage Holdings (OTCQX:ACRDF), the fifth-largest publicly traded tenant, doesn't look great. It makes up 6.4% of ABR, has negative free cash flow, a large debt load, and not much cash. However, it was acquired by Canopy USA, so it can potentially be stronger as a combined company.

The last one I'll talk about is Ayr Wellness (OTCQX:AYRWF), representing 5.8% of ABR as of Q3. This company had -$9.82 million in FCF for the past 12 months, with an interest coverage ratio of -0.3x. Again, though, this is another company where analysts expect improvements. Three analysts estimate its 2025 free cash flow to reach $9.6 million. It's a mixed picture.

As you can see, NLCP doesn't have the best tenants, but my analysis leads me to believe that the top three tenants, which make up 46.7% of ABR, won't have trouble making their rent payments anytime soon, unless analysts' estimates are way off. If you're comfortable with concentration risk and tenant risk, then buying NLCP stock at the current valuation can potentially be rewarding.

NLCP Stock Yields Nearly 11%, Trades Under Tangible Book Value

Now, let's talk about the dividend and the valuation. At current levels, it yields about 11%, with an annualized dividend of $1.72/share. Additionally, the dividend is covered by AFFO, as the AFFO payout ratio was 84% in Q3. This gives the company a decent amount of room to maneuver any drop in earnings without having to cut the dividend.

If the company can maintain this dividend, which is possible since the top tenants aren't bad and the 84% AFFO payout ratio gives some wiggle room for the bad ones, then 11% returns are pretty decent. And if the company grows over time as the cannabis market grows, then the dividend can potentially slowly increase over the years. After all, if we use this conservative estimate from Statista, the US cannabis market is expected to grow at a 2.24% CAGR from 2025-29. That's not much, but it's still growth, and it can help sustain companies like NLCP over the long run.

Moving on to its tangible book value. Per FinBox, NLCP's tangible book value per share is $18.59. That means that the stock trades at 0.84x tangible book value if using its last share price of $15.69. And if you use NLCP's book value per share (not tangible) of $19.48, then the stock trades at 0.81x this level. Similar to the AFFO payout ratio at 84%, this valuation gives you a margin of safety, allowing you to buy the stock under its tangible net worth.

The Takeaway

While NewLake Capital Partners isn't the "safest" investment, it's a high-yield way to get exposure to the growing cannabis market. Not all of NLCP's tenants are financially sound, and some are in trouble. However, the top three tenants, which make up almost half of annualized rent, are not bad. With the stock yielding 11% (covered by AFFO) and now trading at 0.84x tangible book value and 0.81x "regular" book value, I think that a good chunk of the downside risk is priced in.

I don't recommend going big on this stock yet (I'd prefer an even bigger margin of safety), but it's good enough to warrant a Buy rating for those who want a double-digit yield and are comfortable with the risks I've presented.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
